title: Unusual half-metallic state in unconventional magnets
abstract: Half-metallic ferromagnets exhibit a gap in the density of states for one spin projection while remaining gapless for the opposite spin. We show that in helimagnets an unusual half-metallic state can exist, where the spin projection that experiences the gap is determined by the direction of the wave vector. This state originates from the nontrivial topology of the band structure, specifically from the dispersion forming a multi-sheeted covering over the Brillouin zone. We present two-dimensional tight-binding models for $p$-wave and $f$-wave half metals. These structures can be realized in crystalline and van der Waals systems. The complex band structure of the unusual $p$-wave half metal in nanostructures is also discussed. In a quantum well, a standing wave with a helical spin structure is formed, and a persistent spin current exists.
summary: This paper theoretically explores an unusual half-metallic state found in unconventional magnets with helical magnetic order. It develops tight-binding models for p-wave and f-wave states, showing that these topological features lead to unique spin polarization properties. The work predicts the formation of persistent spin currents when these materials are confined into quantum well structures.
main_problem: The study investigates an unusual half-metallic state where the spin gap depends on the direction of the wave vector, originating from the nontrivial topology of the band structure.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that such p-wave and f-wave half-metals can exist in helimagnets and lead to phenomena like standing waves with helical spin structures and persistent spin currents in quantum wells.
method: Theoretical analysis using two-dimensional tight-binding models, analyzing band structure along specific paths in the magnetic Brillouin zone, and reducing the problem to 1D motion for nanostructures.
model_or_system: The model uses a general 2D tight-binding Hamiltonian applied to helimagnets exhibiting $120^\circ$ magnetic order. The system is analyzed in both bulk (rectangular/hexagonal lattices) and confined geometries (quantum wells).
key_observables: Density of States (DOS), spin polarization, band structure visualization, persistent spin current.
important_parameters: The existence of the half-metallic gap depends on the Fermi level placement relative to topological gaps; $120^\circ$ magnetic ordering is crucial.
main_assumptions: For nanostructures, the analysis assumes an infinite potential wall perpendicular to the x-axis. The unusual state requires breaking parity-time symmetry (alternamagnets).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate DOS comparisons between ordinary and unusual half-metals; band structures along X'-$\Gamma$-X paths for $120^\circ$ order on rectangular/hexagonal lattices; and schematics showing the resulting persistent spin current in a quantum well.
paper_structure: The paper first establishes the theoretical framework using tight-binding models for p-wave and f-wave half metals in bulk helimagnets. It then focuses on confinement effects, analyzing the resulting 1D physics in quantum wells to predict observable phenomena like persistent spin currents.
why_interesting: While primarily condensed matter magnetism/spintronics, the focus on topological band structures and emergent spin currents in confined geometries touches upon concepts relevant to engineered quantum systems and boundary physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: (A) A schematic view of the DOS for the ordinary (s-wave) half-metallic ferromagnets. (B) The same for the 1D p-wave half metal. The horizontal arrows denote the left- and right-moving electrons. (C) A spin-split band. (D) A pair of topologically nontrivial bands.

Figure 2: (A) Rectangular lattice with 120◦magnetic order. The sublattices are shown by color and numbers. The supercell is indicated by the ﬁlled rectangle. (B) Band structure along the X’-Γ-X path (t = 0.1, |hi| = h0 = 0.5). The average spin projection onto the z-axis is indicated by color: the red and blue for predominantly spin-up and spin-down states, respectively; the green denotes strongly mixed states, i.e., when |⟨ˆτz⟩| < 1/2. (C) The Fermi surface for the Fermi level at EF = −0.75 (p-wave half-metallic state). The DOS for the left- and right-moving electrons is schematically shown. The green and purple arrows show the backward and umklapp scattering vectors, respectively.

Figure 3: (A) Triangular lattice with 120◦magnetic order shown in the same manner as in Fig. 2A. (B) Band structure along the path shown in the inset (h0 = 1). The average spin projection is indicated as in Fig. 2B. (C) Fermi surface for the Fermi level at EF = −0.6 (f-wave half-metallic state). The DOS for electrons moving along diﬀerent directions is schematically shown.

Figure 4: (A) Schematic view of an inﬁnite potential wall for the tight-binding rectangular model. The site colors denote the superlattices, the black color indicates the sites under the inﬁnite potential. (B) Schematic view of the quantum well. (C) Persistent spin current in the quantum well. The helical spin structure in the central part of the quantum well is shown.
